Highway On-Ramp Merge.text: Vehicle 1 travels along a single mainline highway lane. Vehicle 2 travels along a single-lane on-ramp that runs parallel to Vehicle 1's lane for a short approach before converging into the mainline. The vehicles enter the merge region with overlapping timing, creating an ambiguous merge interaction.

Vehicle 1 travels along a single mainline highway lane. Vehicle 2 travels along a single-lane on-ramp that runs parallel to the mainline before converging into it. Vehicle 3 travels behind Vehicle 1 in the same mainline lane within the approach region, reducing available spacing while Vehicle 2 reaches the merge region.

Vehicle 1 travels along a single mainline highway lane. Vehicles 2 and 3 travel on the same single-lane on-ramp, spaced within the ramp approach region, and the ramp runs parallel to the mainline before converging into it. As the ramp lane ends, Vehicles 2 and 3 reach the merge region while Vehicle 1 is nearby in the mainline lane, creating back-to-back merge conflicts without requiring tight packing.

Vehicles 1, 3, and 4 travel along a single mainline highway lane in that order with feasible headways. Vehicle 2 travels along a single-lane on-ramp that runs parallel to the mainline before converging into it. The merge region is reached while the mainline has an existing queue, creating limited space for adjustment during the merge interaction.

Vehicle 1 travels along a single mainline highway lane. Vehicle 2 travels along a single-lane on-ramp that runs parallel to the mainline before converging into it. Vehicle 3 is ahead of Vehicle 1 in the same mainline lane within the downstream region after the merge, and Vehicle 4 is behind Vehicle 1 within the upstream approach region. This creates a merge interaction where Vehicle 1 has limited room to adjust both before and after the merge without relying on unrealistic initial spacing.

Lane Drop Merge (Zipper).text: Vehicle 1 travels straight in a two-lane road segment (same direction). Vehicle 2 travels straight in the adjacent lane (same direction). The two lanes converge into a single lane (lane drop) and both vehicles reach the merge taper with overlapping timing, creating an ambiguous zipper-style merge interaction.

Vehicle 1 travels straight in the continuing lane of a two-lane same-direction road segment that drops to one lane. Vehicle 2 travels straight in the dropping lane toward the taper. Vehicle 3 follows behind Vehicle 2 in the dropping lane within the approach region, adding pressure during the merge without requiring tight headways.

Vehicle 1 travels straight in the dropping lane of a two-lane same-direction road segment that converges into one lane. Vehicle 2 travels straight in the continuing lane. Vehicle 3 is ahead of Vehicle 2 in the continuing lane within the downstream merge region, creating limited gaps while Vehicle 1 reaches the taper.

A two-lane same-direction road segment drops to one lane. Vehicles 1 and 3 travel straight in the dropping lane in that order with feasible spacing. Vehicles 2 and 4 travel straight in the continuing lane in that order with feasible spacing. The four vehicles reach the taper region close enough in time to create multiple plausible merge orders without requiring bumper-to-bumper starts.

A two-lane same-direction road segment drops to one lane shortly after a gentle curve that limits long-range visibility. Vehicle 1 travels in the dropping lane and Vehicle 2 travels in the continuing lane toward the taper. Vehicle 3 follows Vehicle 1 in the dropping lane, and Vehicles 4 and 5 occupy the continuing lane ahead and behind Vehicle 2 within the approach region. The lane drop occurs in a constrained visibility context, increasing negotiation complexity at the taper.

Roundabout Entry.text: Vehicle 1 approaches a single-lane roundabout entry and intends to enter the circulating lane. Vehicle 2 is already circulating in the single roundabout lane and approaches Vehicle 1's entry conflict region. Their arrival windows overlap near the entry point, creating ambiguous entry timing without requiring exact synchronization.

Vehicle 1 approaches a single-lane roundabout from one entry and intends to enter the circulating lane. Vehicle 2 approaches the same roundabout from a different entry and also intends to enter. Vehicle 3 is circulating in the roundabout lane between the two entries, so both entries face overlapping conflicts with circulating traffic.

Vehicle 1 approaches a single-lane roundabout entry and intends to enter. Vehicle 2 is circulating and intends to exit at an exit adjacent to Vehicle 1's entry region. Vehicle 3 is circulating behind Vehicle 2 in the same roundabout lane. The entry region must be negotiated while an exit maneuver occurs nearby in the circulating lane.

Vehicles 1, 2, and 3 approach a single-lane roundabout from three different entries and intend to enter the circulating lane. Vehicle 4 is already circulating in the roundabout lane and passes by at least one of the entry conflict regions during the approach. The interaction creates multiple overlapping entry conflicts without assuming any fixed yielding outcome.

Vehicle 1 approaches a single-lane roundabout entry and intends to enter. Vehicles 2 and 3 are circulating in the roundabout lane with feasible spacing, forming a short circulating queue past the entry. Vehicles 4 and 5 approach from two other entries and intend to enter as well. The scenario creates layered conflicts between circulating traffic and multiple entry attempts, while remaining within single-lane roundabout topology.

Courtesy & Deadlock Negotiation.text: Vehicle 1 approaches an uncontrolled four-way intersection and intends to continue straight. Vehicle 2 approaches from a perpendicular leg and also intends to continue straight. Their approach windows overlap near the junction center, creating ambiguous right-of-way negotiation without assuming stop-sign or signal semantics.

Vehicle 1 approaches an uncontrolled intersection intending to continue straight. Vehicle 2 approaches from a perpendicular leg intending to turn right into Vehicle 1's road. Vehicle 3 follows behind Vehicle 1 in the same approach lane within the approach region, adding pressure on Vehicle 1's ability to adjust while Vehicle 2 approaches the conflict region.

Vehicles 1, 2, 3, and 4 each approach an uncontrolled four-way intersection from different legs. Vehicle 1 and Vehicle 3 intend to continue straight through, while Vehicle 2 intends to turn right and Vehicle 4 intends to turn left. Their approach windows overlap around the intersection, creating ambiguous priority across multiple crossing and turning conflicts.

Vehicle 1 approaches an uncontrolled intersection intending to turn right. Vehicle 2 approaches from the perpendicular leg intending to continue straight through the intersection. Vehicle 3 approaches from behind Vehicle 1 in the same lane within the approach region. A pedestrian crosses the crosswalk on Vehicle 1's destination leg near the corner, creating an additional conflict near the turn exit region.

Vehicle 1 approaches an uncontrolled intersection intending to continue straight. Vehicle 2 approaches from a perpendicular leg intending to continue straight. Vehicle 3 is positioned ahead of Vehicle 1 on the downstream lane after the intersection within the exit region, constraining downstream space. Vehicle 4 approaches from behind Vehicle 2 in the same lane. The intersection negotiation occurs while downstream space is limited, increasing planning complexity without requiring a standstill gridlock assumption.

Unprotected Left Turn.text: Vehicle 1 approaches a standard intersection intending to turn left across opposing traffic into a destination road. Vehicle 2 approaches from the opposing direction intending to continue straight through the intersection. Their arrival windows overlap near the left-turn conflict zone, creating an unprotected left-turn negotiation.

Vehicle 1 approaches an intersection intending to turn left across opposing traffic. Vehicle 2 approaches from the opposing direction intending to continue straight. Vehicle 3 follows behind Vehicle 1 in the same approach lane within the approach region, reducing available spacing for Vehicle 1's adjustments as the left-turn conflict develops.

Vehicle 1 approaches an intersection intending to turn left across opposing traffic. Vehicles 2 and 3 approach from the opposing direction in the same oncoming lane with feasible spacing, both intending to continue straight through. The left-turn conflict zone is reached while the oncoming stream is nearby, creating a more complex gap negotiation without prescribing any outcome.

Vehicle 1 approaches an intersection intending to turn left across oncoming traffic into a destination road. Vehicle 2 approaches from the opposing direction intending to continue straight. Vehicle 3 approaches from the cross street intending to continue straight across the intersection, and Vehicle 4 follows behind Vehicle 3 in the same lane. Vehicle 1 must negotiate overlapping conflicts from oncoming and cross traffic around the left-turn region.

Vehicle 1 approaches an intersection intending to turn left across oncoming traffic. Vehicle 2 approaches from the opposing direction intending to continue straight. Vehicle 3 is a large vehicle ahead of Vehicle 2 in the oncoming lane within the approach region, partially occluding Vehicle 2 from Vehicle 1 until later in the approach. A pedestrian crosses the crosswalk on Vehicle 1's destination leg near the corner, adding a second conflict after the turn entry.

Lane Change Negotiation.text: Vehicle 1 travels straight on a multi-lane same-direction road and intends to move into the adjacent lane. Vehicle 2 travels in that adjacent lane alongside Vehicle 1 within the same road segment. The vehicles are positioned so the lane change decision must be negotiated without relying on exact side-by-side alignment.

Vehicle 1 travels straight on a multi-lane same-direction road and intends to move into the adjacent lane. Vehicle 2 travels in the target lane slightly ahead within the approach region. Vehicle 3 travels in the target lane behind Vehicle 2, creating a constrained gap for Vehicle 1's lane change without requiring very tight spacing.

Vehicle 1 travels straight on a multi-lane same-direction road and intends to move into the adjacent lane. Vehicle 2 travels in the target lane ahead within the same region, and Vehicle 3 travels in the target lane behind within the same region. Vehicle 4 follows behind Vehicle 1 in its current lane. The lane change must be negotiated with limited available gaps both in the target lane and in Vehicle 1's current lane.

Vehicles 1 and 2 travel straight in adjacent lanes on a multi-lane same-direction road. Vehicle 1 intends to move into Vehicle 2's lane while Vehicle 2 intends to move into Vehicle 1's lane within the same region. Vehicle 3 travels behind Vehicle 2 in its lane, creating additional constraint on how the simultaneous lane-change intentions can be resolved by planners.

A multi-lane same-direction road segment approaches a lane drop where one lane ends and merges into the other. Vehicle 1 travels in the lane that continues and intends to move into the adjacent lane before the lane drop. Vehicle 2 travels in the adjacent lane that will drop, and Vehicle 3 travels behind Vehicle 2 in the dropping lane. Vehicle 4 travels behind Vehicle 1 in the continuing lane. This creates overlapping lane-change negotiations while the lane topology is about to change.

Highway Weaving.text: Vehicle 1 and Vehicle 2 travel along a multi-lane highway segment in the same direction. Vehicle 1 travels in the right lane and Vehicle 2 travels in the left lane, with their longitudinal positions close enough to create interaction. The scenario targets weaving-style negotiation as each vehicle maintains its route within the highway segment.

Vehicle 1 travels on a multi-lane highway segment in the right lane. Vehicle 2 travels in the left lane near Vehicle 1 within the same region. Vehicle 3 travels behind Vehicle 2 in the left lane within the approach region, creating a constrained environment for any lane adjustments while the vehicles remain on the highway segment.

A multi-lane highway segment includes a rightmost lane that diverges into an exit. Vehicle 1 travels in the middle lane and intends to reach the rightmost lane before the diverge. Vehicle 2 travels in the rightmost lane near Vehicle 1 within the same region, and Vehicle 3 travels in the middle lane behind Vehicle 1. The vehicles must negotiate lane positioning as the diverge approaches, without prescribing any specific merge order.

Vehicles 1 and 2 travel along a three-lane same-direction highway segment in adjacent lanes near each other. Vehicle 3 occupies the third lane within the same region, and Vehicle 4 travels behind Vehicle 2 in its lane. The distribution across three lanes creates multiple plausible lane-change negotiations while all vehicles remain within the highway segment.

A highway segment includes a rightmost lane that diverges toward an exit, and shortly before the diverge, one of the non-exit lanes drops and merges into an adjacent lane. Vehicle 1 intends to stay on the mainline through the lane-drop region, while Vehicle 2 intends to reach the exit lane. Vehicle 3 occupies the exit lane near Vehicle 2, and Vehicles 4 and 5 occupy the lanes involved in the lane drop. The scenario creates overlapping weaving and merge interactions under changing lane topology.

Overtaking on Two-Lane Road.text: Vehicle 1 travels straight on a road segment that has two same-direction lanes (a passing lane). Vehicle 2 travels ahead of Vehicle 1 in the right lane. Vehicle 1 approaches the region where passing in the left lane is possible, creating an overtaking decision without requiring use of an oncoming lane.

Vehicle 1 travels behind Vehicle 2 in the right lane on a segment with a same-direction passing lane to the left. Vehicle 3 is already traveling in the left passing lane near the overtaking region. Vehicle 1 reaches the passing region while Vehicle 3 is nearby in the left lane, creating ambiguity about whether an overtake is available.

A same-direction passing lane exists to the left of the right lane and then ends, merging back into a single lane. Vehicle 1 travels behind Vehicle 2 in the right lane and approaches the passing lane entrance. Vehicle 3 is in the left passing lane near the region where the passing lane ends. The scenario combines overtaking intent with a downstream lane-drop merge.

Vehicle 1 travels behind Vehicle 2 in the right lane on a segment with a same-direction left passing lane. Vehicle 3 is a large vehicle in the left passing lane ahead within the region, partially occluding other left-lane traffic. Vehicle 4 travels behind Vehicle 3 in the left lane. Vehicle 1 reaches the overtaking region while left-lane occupancy is partially occluded, increasing overtaking decision complexity without relying on oncoming traffic.

Vehicle 1 travels behind Vehicle 2 in the right lane on a segment with a same-direction left passing lane. An upcoming intersection requires Vehicle 1 to continue straight through after the passing region. Vehicle 3 occupies the left lane near the passing region, and Vehicle 4 travels behind Vehicle 1 in the right lane. Vehicle 1 must negotiate whether to attempt an overtake within the limited passing region before reaching the intersection approach.

Opposing Traffic on Narrow Road.text: Vehicle 1 and Vehicle 2 travel on a two-lane undivided road in opposite directions (one lane each direction). The road includes a short bridge segment where the lanes remain separate but the usable lateral clearance is reduced. The vehicles approach the bridge segment from opposite sides with overlapping timing, creating a cautious negotiation about entering the constrained segment.

Vehicle 1 and Vehicle 2 travel in opposite directions on a two-lane undivided road. A short constrained segment (e.g., narrow bridge) preserves one lane per direction but reduces lateral clearance. Vehicle 3 follows behind Vehicle 1 in the same lane within the approach region, adding pressure on Vehicle 1's approach to the constrained segment as Vehicle 2 approaches from the other side.

Vehicle 1 and Vehicle 2 travel in opposite directions on a two-lane undivided road. Near the entrance of a constrained segment that preserves one lane per direction, Vehicle 3 is parked along the roadside adjacent to Vehicle 1's lane, reducing usable lateral space at the entrance. Vehicle 1 and Vehicle 2 approach the constriction with overlapping timing, creating additional caution and negotiation near the entrance region.

Vehicle 1 and Vehicle 2 travel in opposite directions on a two-lane undivided road approaching a short constrained segment (one lane per direction). Vehicle 3 approaches from a side street near the constrained segment and intends to turn right into Vehicle 1's direction of travel. The vehicles must negotiate entry into the constrained area while a side-street merge occurs nearby.

Vehicle 1 and Vehicle 2 travel in opposite directions on a two-lane undivided road approaching a short constrained segment that preserves one lane per direction. A pedestrian (non-ego) crosses at a crosswalk located near one end of the constrained segment on Vehicle 1's side. Vehicle 3 follows behind Vehicle 2 in its lane. The vehicles reach the region where the crosswalk and constriction interact, creating layered caution and negotiation without requiring lane borrowing or reversing.

Pedestrian Crosswalk.text: Vehicle 1 travels straight along a road segment that includes a marked or implied crosswalk. A pedestrian (non-ego) crosses the roadway in front of Vehicle 1 within the approach region, creating a straightforward yield interaction.

Vehicle 1 travels straight in the right lane of a two-lane same-direction road segment that includes a crosswalk ahead. Vehicle 2 travels straight in the left lane near Vehicle 1 within the same region. A pedestrian (non-ego) crosses at the crosswalk, creating a coordination problem between lanes without relying on exact stopping positions.

Vehicle 1 travels straight toward a crosswalk. A parked van is positioned near the curb before the crosswalk on Vehicle 1's right side, partially occluding a pedestrian (non-ego) as they begin to cross. Vehicle 1 reaches the crosswalk approach region while the pedestrian enters the crossing from behind the occluder.

Vehicle 1 approaches an intersection intending to turn right. Vehicle 2 approaches from the perpendicular leg intending to continue straight through. A pedestrian (non-ego) crosses the crosswalk on Vehicle 1's destination leg near the corner. The turning maneuver and the crossing vehicle interaction overlap in the same region, creating an ambiguous negotiation.

Vehicle 1 travels straight toward a crosswalk on a multi-lane same-direction road. Vehicle 2 travels in the adjacent lane near the crosswalk region, and Vehicle 3 follows behind Vehicle 2 in that lane. A parked vehicle near the curb partially occludes a pedestrian (non-ego) entering the crosswalk. Vehicle 1 reaches the crosswalk approach region while adjacent-lane traffic and occlusion increase complexity.

Occluded Hazard.text: Vehicle 1 approaches an intersection intending to continue straight. Vehicle 2 approaches from a perpendicular leg intending to continue straight across. A parked box truck near the corner partially occludes Vehicle 2 from Vehicle 1 until late in the approach region, creating a late-reveal crossing conflict.

Vehicle 1 approaches an intersection intending to turn left across oncoming traffic. Vehicle 2 approaches from the opposing direction intending to continue straight. Vehicle 3 is ahead of Vehicle 2 in the oncoming lane within the approach region, partially occluding Vehicle 2 from Vehicle 1. The left-turn decision is made under partial occlusion without requiring any specific yielding outcome.

Vehicle 1 travels straight behind Vehicle 2 on a single-lane road segment. Vehicle 2 is a large vehicle that partially occludes the view of a stopped obstacle or parked vehicle ahead in Vehicle 2's lane. Vehicle 1 reaches the region where the obstacle becomes visible, creating a late-reveal hazard while remaining within a single lane topology.

Vehicle 1 travels straight on a multi-lane same-direction road and approaches an intersection intending to continue straight. Vehicle 2 travels in the adjacent lane slightly ahead, partially occluding Vehicle 1's view of Vehicle 3 approaching from a perpendicular leg. Vehicle 3 intends to cross straight through the intersection. The crossing conflict emerges under partial occlusion caused by nearby adjacent-lane traffic.

A two-lane same-direction road segment drops to one lane. Vehicle 1 travels in the dropping lane and Vehicle 2 travels in the continuing lane near the taper. Vehicle 3 is a large vehicle ahead of Vehicle 2 in the continuing lane, partially occluding the taper region from Vehicle 1's perspective. Vehicle 4 follows behind Vehicle 1 in the dropping lane. The lane drop must be negotiated while visibility of the merge taper is reduced by occlusion.

Blocked Lane (Obstacle).text: Vehicle 1 travels straight in the right lane of a two-lane same-direction road. A stopped vehicle is positioned ahead in Vehicle 1's lane, blocking that lane segment. Vehicle 2 travels straight in the left lane near the region of the blockage. Vehicle 1 must negotiate a lane change around the blocked lane segment using existing adjacent-lane topology.

Vehicle 1 travels in the right lane of a two-lane same-direction road toward a stopped vehicle blocking its lane. Vehicle 2 travels in the left lane near the blockage region, and Vehicle 3 travels behind Vehicle 2 in the left lane within the approach region. The lane change around the obstruction must be negotiated with adjacent-lane traffic present.

A two-lane same-direction road segment contains two stopped vehicles: one blocks the right lane in an upstream region, and another blocks the left lane in a downstream region. Vehicle 1 begins in the right lane, Vehicle 2 begins in the left lane near Vehicle 1, and Vehicle 3 follows behind Vehicle 1 in the right lane. The vehicles must negotiate multiple lane adjustments across two obstruction regions without relying on off-road detours.

Vehicle 1 approaches an intersection intending to continue straight in the right lane of a two-lane same-direction road. A stopped vehicle blocks Vehicle 1's lane in the approach region before the intersection. Vehicle 2 travels in the adjacent left lane toward the same intersection. Vehicle 3 approaches from a perpendicular leg intending to cross, and Vehicle 4 follows behind Vehicle 2 in the left lane. The obstruction forces lane negotiation while an intersection conflict also exists ahead.

Vehicle 1 travels in the right lane of a two-lane same-direction road toward a stopped vehicle that blocks its lane. Vehicle 2 travels in the left lane near the blockage region. After passing the obstruction, the road returns to a single lane near a crosswalk where a pedestrian (non-ego) is crossing. Vehicle 3 follows behind Vehicle 1 in the right lane. The vehicles must negotiate both the lane change around the obstruction and the subsequent merge back while a crosswalk conflict exists nearby.

Construction Zone.text: A two-lane same-direction road segment has a construction cone taper that closes the right lane, merging traffic into the left lane. Vehicle 1 travels in the closing right lane and Vehicle 2 travels in the left lane near the taper region. The scenario creates a merge negotiation at the cone taper without assuming any specific merge order.

A construction zone closes the right lane of a two-lane same-direction road via a cone taper. A parked work truck is positioned within the closed lane beyond the taper. Vehicle 1 travels in the closing lane toward the taper, Vehicle 2 travels in the open lane near the taper, and Vehicle 3 follows behind Vehicle 2 in the open lane. The presence of the work vehicle increases urgency while remaining within existing lane topology.

A construction cone taper closes one lane of a two-lane same-direction road segment. Shortly downstream, the remaining two-lane segment transitions into a single lane due to a lane drop. Vehicle 1 starts in the closing lane, Vehicle 2 starts in the open lane, Vehicle 3 follows behind Vehicle 1, and Vehicle 4 is ahead of Vehicle 2. The scenario creates two successive merge pressures under construction constraints.

A construction zone narrows a same-direction road segment from two lanes to one using a cone taper, located immediately before a standard lane-drop merge region. Vehicle 1 travels in the lane that will close, Vehicle 2 travels in the adjacent lane, and Vehicle 3 approaches from a side street and merges into the same road segment upstream of the construction. The vehicles must negotiate merging and lane reduction in close proximity while staying within lane-marked topology.

A construction zone performs a lane shift: cones guide the right lane to merge left while a barrier occupies part of the right side, keeping the road two lanes but with reduced lateral margin in one lane. Vehicle 1 travels in the affected lane, Vehicle 2 travels alongside in the adjacent lane, and Vehicles 3 and 4 occupy positions ahead and behind in the adjacent lane within the approach region. The scenario stresses negotiation during a lane shift without introducing detours or contraflow.

Multi-Conflict Scenarios.text: Vehicle 1 approaches an intersection intending to turn left across oncoming traffic. Vehicle 2 approaches from the opposing direction intending to continue straight. A pedestrian (non-ego) crosses the crosswalk on Vehicle 1's destination leg near the corner. Vehicle 1 must account for both the left-turn conflict and the downstream crosswalk conflict in the same region.

A two-lane same-direction approach to an intersection drops to one lane before the stop line. Vehicle 1 travels in the dropping lane and Vehicle 2 travels in the continuing lane toward the intersection. Vehicle 3 approaches from a perpendicular leg intending to continue straight across. The lane-drop merge and the intersection crossing conflict occur in close proximity, creating layered negotiation.

Vehicle 1 travels straight on a main road segment. Vehicle 2 approaches from a side street intending to turn right into Vehicle 1's lane. A pedestrian (non-ego) crosses at a crosswalk located shortly downstream of the merge point. Vehicle 3 follows behind Vehicle 1 in the main lane within the approach region. The merge and crosswalk conflicts overlap in the same downstream region.

Vehicle 1 travels straight on a multi-lane same-direction road and intends to move into the adjacent lane within the approach region. Vehicle 2 travels in the target lane near Vehicle 1. Vehicle 3 approaches a perpendicular intersection intending to cross straight through, and a parked truck near the corner partially occludes Vehicle 3 from Vehicle 1. Vehicle 4 follows behind Vehicle 1 in its current lane. The lane-change negotiation occurs while an occluded intersection crossing conflict also emerges.

A two-lane same-direction road approaches an intersection and drops to one lane shortly before the junction. Vehicle 1 travels in the dropping lane and Vehicle 2 travels in the continuing lane toward the taper. Vehicle 3 approaches from the opposing direction intending to go straight through the intersection, and Vehicle 4 approaches from a perpendicular leg intending to cross. A parked box truck near one corner partially occludes one of the crossing approaches. Vehicle 5 follows behind Vehicle 1 in the dropping lane within the approach region. The scenario combines a lane-drop merge, occlusion, and multi-direction intersection conflicts.

Side Street Entry.text: Vehicle 1 approaches from a side street and intends to turn right into a main road lane. Vehicle 2 travels along that main road lane toward the entry point. The side street join is a standard merge into an existing lane, creating an ambiguous entry gap negotiation.

Vehicle 1 approaches from a side street intending to turn right into a main road lane. Vehicle 2 travels along the main road lane toward the entry point, and Vehicle 3 follows behind Vehicle 2 in the same lane within the approach region. Vehicle 1 reaches the merge point while the main lane contains a short stream, increasing entry difficulty without requiring tight spacing.

Vehicle 1 approaches from a side street intending to turn right onto a main road segment that has two same-direction lanes. Vehicle 2 travels in the right lane toward the entry region, and Vehicle 3 travels in the left lane near Vehicle 2. Vehicle 1 reaches the entry region where both lanes are occupied, creating ambiguity about which lane to target for a safe merge within existing topology.

Vehicle 1 turns right from a side street into a main road with two same-direction lanes. Downstream, one lane drops and merges into the other. Vehicle 2 travels in the lane that will drop, Vehicle 3 travels in the lane that continues, and Vehicle 4 follows behind Vehicle 3. Vehicle 1 enters the main road in the region upstream of the lane drop, creating interacting merge negotiations between the side-street entry and the upcoming lane reduction.

Vehicle 1 approaches from a side street intending to turn right into a main road lane. A parked van near the corner partially occludes the view of a pedestrian (non-ego) crossing the crosswalk that spans the side street mouth. Vehicle 2 travels along the main road lane toward the entry point, and Vehicle 3 follows behind Vehicle 2. Vehicle 1 must negotiate both the pedestrian conflict at the corner and the merge into main road traffic.

Emergency Vehicle Encounter.text: Vehicle 1 travels straight in a single lane. Vehicle 2 travels behind Vehicle 1 in the same lane within the approach region and is a priority vehicle whose route continues forward past Vehicle 1. The vehicles interact as Vehicle 2 closes the gap from behind, creating ambiguity about how Vehicle 1 should yield within the existing lane topology.

Vehicle 1 travels in the right lane of a two-lane same-direction road. Vehicle 2 (priority vehicle) travels behind Vehicle 1 in the same lane within the approach region. Vehicle 3 travels in the adjacent left lane near the region, constraining lateral options as the priority vehicle approaches from behind. The interaction requires negotiation without assuming any special roadway beyond standard lanes.

Vehicle 1 approaches an uncontrolled intersection intending to continue straight. Vehicle 2 (priority vehicle) approaches from behind in the same lane and also intends to continue straight. Vehicle 3 approaches from a perpendicular leg intending to cross straight through. The intersection conflict overlaps with the rear-approach priority interaction, creating a layered negotiation.

A two-lane same-direction road segment drops to one lane. Vehicle 1 travels in the continuing lane toward the taper. Vehicle 2 (priority vehicle) travels behind Vehicle 1 in the continuing lane within the approach region. Vehicle 3 travels in the dropping lane near the taper. The priority interaction occurs while a lane-drop merge conflict is also present.

A multi-lane highway segment includes a rightmost exit lane that diverges. Vehicle 1 travels in the right lane intending to remain on the mainline. Vehicle 2 (priority vehicle) travels behind Vehicle 1 in the same lane. Vehicle 3 occupies the adjacent lane near the region, and Vehicle 4 occupies the exit lane near the diverge. The priority interaction overlaps with diverge-related lane positioning constraints, creating complex negotiation within standard lane topology.

Wide Turn Negotiation.text: Vehicle 1 is a large vehicle approaching an intersection in the rightmost lane intending to turn right. Vehicle 2 travels straight in the adjacent lane (same direction) approaching the same intersection. The turn geometry and vehicle size create ambiguity about available lateral space during the right turn without requiring centerline crossing.

Vehicle 1 is a large vehicle in the rightmost lane approaching an intersection intending to turn right. Vehicle 2 travels straight in the adjacent lane approaching the same intersection. Vehicle 3 follows behind Vehicle 1 in the rightmost lane within the approach region, adding pressure on how Vehicle 1 positions for the turn while adjacent-lane traffic is present.

Vehicle 1 is a large vehicle approaching an intersection intending to turn right from the rightmost lane. Vehicle 2 approaches from behind in the adjacent lane intending to continue straight. A pedestrian (non-ego) crosses the crosswalk on Vehicle 1's destination leg near the corner. Vehicle 1 must negotiate lateral space for the wide turn while also accounting for the corner crosswalk conflict.

Vehicle 1 is a large vehicle approaching an intersection intending to turn right. Vehicle 2 is another large vehicle approaching from the perpendicular leg intending to turn left into Vehicle 1's road. Vehicle 3 travels straight in the lane adjacent to Vehicle 1 on its approach. The scenario creates overlapping swept-path uncertainty between two turning large vehicles while keeping all maneuvers within standard lanes.

Vehicle 1 is a large vehicle approaching an intersection intending to turn right into a road segment that quickly drops from two same-direction lanes to one. Vehicle 2 travels straight in the adjacent lane approaching the intersection. Vehicle 3 occupies the destination road's lane that will drop within the downstream region, and Vehicle 4 occupies the lane that continues. The wide turn must be negotiated while downstream lane topology changes constrain available space after the turn.

Shared Left Turn Lane Conflict.text: Vehicle 1 approaches an intersection from one direction intending to turn left into a narrow side street. Vehicle 2 approaches from the opposite direction also intending to turn left into the same side street. Each vehicle has its own left-turn pocket in its approach direction, but the side street entrance is narrow enough that their turn entries interact, creating ambiguous ordering at the side street mouth.

Vehicle 1 and Vehicle 2 approach from opposite directions and both intend to turn left into the same side street from their respective left-turn pockets. Vehicle 3 travels on the side street intending to exit into the main road through the same mouth region. The side street entrance becomes a shared conflict zone among the two left turns and the side-street exit.

Vehicle 1 approaches intending to turn left into a side street and Vehicle 3 follows behind Vehicle 1 in the same left-turn pocket with feasible spacing. Vehicle 2 approaches from the opposite direction also intending to turn left into the same side street, and Vehicle 4 follows behind Vehicle 2 in its left-turn pocket. The side street mouth is the shared conflict region, creating a multi-vehicle left-turn ordering problem while all vehicles remain in their marked lanes.

Vehicle 1 and Vehicle 2 approach from opposite directions and intend to turn left into the same side street from their respective left-turn pockets. Vehicle 3 travels straight through the intersection on the main road. A pedestrian (non-ego) crosses the crosswalk across the side street mouth. The left-turn entries must be negotiated while a crosswalk conflict exists at the shared entrance.

Vehicle 1 and Vehicle 2 approach from opposite directions intending to turn left into the same side street from their own left-turn pockets. A parked van near the side street corner partially occludes the view of the side street mouth for one approach. Vehicle 3 travels straight on the main road adjacent to Vehicle 1's approach, and Vehicle 4 travels straight adjacent to Vehicle 2's approach. The scenario creates an occluded, multi-actor left-turn ordering conflict concentrated at the side street entrance.

Parking Lot Maneuvers.text: Vehicle 1 is positioned at a driveway exit (forward-facing) intending to merge into a narrow access road lane. Vehicle 2 travels along the access road toward the driveway region. The merge occurs at the driveway mouth, creating a low-speed negotiation within a lane-marked access-road network.

A narrow access road has two opposing driveway exits. Vehicle 1 intends to enter the access road from one driveway while Vehicle 2 intends to enter from the opposite driveway. Vehicle 3 travels along the access road between them. The interaction creates ambiguity about gap acceptance at two closely spaced driveway mouths.

Vehicle 1 travels along a narrow access road approaching a T-junction intending to turn right. Vehicle 2 approaches the same T-junction from the perpendicular access road intending to turn left into Vehicle 1's road. Vehicle 3 is positioned at a driveway exit near the junction intending to merge into one of the access roads. The scenario creates a multi-conflict low-speed negotiation in a lane-marked access-road network.

Vehicle 1 is stopped along the curb within a lane-marked access road segment and intends to reenter the lane to continue forward. Vehicle 2 travels along the same access road lane approaching Vehicle 1 from behind. Vehicle 3 is positioned at a nearby driveway exit intending to enter the access road. The scenario creates overlapping reentry and driveway-merge negotiations without requiring any parking-spot trajectories.

A lane-marked access road segment has multiple driveway connections in a short region. Vehicle 1 intends to exit from a driveway into the access road. Vehicle 2 travels along the access road toward Vehicle 1's exit, and Vehicle 3 travels in the same direction in an adjacent lane if present or follows behind Vehicle 2 within the approach region. Vehicle 4 intends to turn from the access road into another driveway shortly downstream. A pedestrian (non-ego) crosses at a crosswalk along the access road in the same region. The scenario creates layered low-speed negotiation among multiple access points and a crosswalk conflict.

Highway Diverge / Off-Ramp Exit Negotiat.text: A multi-lane highway segment includes a rightmost exit lane that diverges into an off-ramp. Vehicle 1 travels in the lane adjacent to the exit lane and intends to reach the exit lane before the diverge. Vehicle 2 travels in the exit lane near the diverge region. Vehicle 1 must negotiate entry into the exit lane within the diverge approach region without assuming any fixed merge outcome.

A highway segment includes a rightmost exit lane that diverges. Vehicle 1 intends to reach the exit lane from an adjacent lane. Vehicle 2 travels in the exit lane near the diverge, and Vehicle 3 travels behind Vehicle 2 in the exit lane within the approach region. The exit-lane entry must be negotiated with both forward and rear constraints in the exit lane.

A highway segment includes a rightmost exit lane that diverges. Vehicle 1 travels in the middle lane and intends to reach the exit lane. Vehicle 2 travels in the lane adjacent to the exit lane and also intends to reach the exit lane. Vehicle 3 occupies the exit lane near the diverge, and Vehicle 4 follows behind Vehicle 2 in its lane. The scenario creates competing exit-lane entry intentions with overlapping timing in the diverge approach region.

A highway segment includes a rightmost exit lane that diverges, but the exit lane begins as a short auxiliary lane that first merges from the mainline and later splits off. Vehicle 1 travels in the adjacent lane intending to enter the auxiliary lane early. Vehicle 2 travels in the auxiliary lane near the region where it begins, and Vehicle 3 travels in the mainline lane that will merge into the auxiliary lane. The scenario creates negotiation at the auxiliary-lane entry while the lane is also being fed by a mainline merge.

A multi-lane highway segment includes a rightmost exit lane that diverges. Vehicle 1 intends to remain on the mainline and therefore prefers not to enter the exit lane. Vehicle 2 intends to enter the exit lane from an adjacent lane. Vehicle 3 is already in the exit lane near the diverge. Vehicle 4 is in the adjacent lane near Vehicle 1, and Vehicle 5 follows behind Vehicle 2 in its lane. The scenario creates a weave where one vehicle aims to avoid the exit lane while another aims to enter it, under nearby lane occupancy constraints.
